text: We present a fifty seven-year-old female with a large hiatal hernia as confirmed on an upper GI study.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer energy device to take down the short gastric vessels along the greater curvature of the stomach.
The surgeon medializes the stomach and dissects the posterior gastric attachments, separating them from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon approaches the left crus of the diaphragm and enters the hernia sac, revealing the anatomical structures involved in the hiatal hernia repair.
The surgeon begins dissection along the lesser curvature of the stomach, carefully separating tissue in this area.
The surgeon identifies the right cruce of the diaphragm and performs blunt dissection to separate the hernia sac from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses blunt dissection to continue medially, extending the left-sided dissection along the stomach and diaphragm.
The surgeon enters the mediastinum and continues the dissection, focusing on separating tissue planes within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the left vagus nerve and continues the mediastinal esophageal dissection, carefully maneuvering around the nerve.
The surgeon takes down the posterior attachments of the stomach, separating them from surrounding structures using blunt dissection.
The surgeon continues the right-sided mediastinal dissection, meticulously avoiding the pleura and the right vagus nerve, while separating the tissue layers.
The surgeon achieves circumferential control of the esophagus, indicating successful dissection around the structure within the mediastinum.
The surgeon continues the posterior dissection in the mediastinum, carefully navigating around the mediastinal aorta to avoid injury.
The surgeon resects the hernia sac, removing it from the surrounding tissue within the surgical field.
An endoscope is inserted, and the stomach is visibly insufflated, expanding as air is introduced.
The surgeon confirms the intra-abdominal location of the gastroesophageal (GE) junction, visually verifying its position within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon begins the primary posterior cruroplasty, placing interrupted Ethabon sutures to approximate the crura.
The surgeon uses the robotic system to tie the sutures intercorporeally, securing the posterior cruroplasty.
The surgeon cuts a 6x9 cm piece of Alloderm mesh into a keyhole configuration and places it into the abdomen, where it is unfurled to cover the hernia repair site.
The endoscope is maneuvered behind the esophagus, providing a view of the posterior aspect of the structure.
The surgeon closes the keyhole portion of the alloderm mesh using Ethabon sutures, securing the mesh in place within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon secures the alloderm mesh in place within the abdomen using four-point fixation, ensuring stable attachment to the surrounding tissue.
